ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic tart class; no lemon-tart-specific URI confirmed. Closest parent class used as defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tarts and flans — pastry-based sweet baked goods
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tarts and flans as a category; no lemon-tart-specific code confirmed. Closest parent category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for lemon tart as a food concept.

main_ingredients: pastry shell (wheat flour, butter, sugar, eggs)
lemon curd (lemon juice, lemon zest, eggs, butter, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lemon tart is one slice or one individual tart, approximately 100 g, providing around 374 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon Tart — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Lemon Tart (cr_id F11110017). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 11 g/100 g based on the known butter-dominant fat profile of lemon tart (total fat 19.57 g/100 g); HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry, so the estimate is derived from USDA FoodData Central reference item 'Tart, lemon' (FDC ID 1100537) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on shortcrust pastry / sweet pastry tart
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for lemon tart was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweet shortcrust pastry tart entries (GI range 50–55) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). GI 55 falls within the low GI range (≤55) per standard classification; gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate) / 100 = (55 × 43.9) / 100 = 24.1 g, which is high GL, reflecting the large carbohydrate portion despite the low-to-moderate GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (24.6 g/100 g) and estimated saturated fat (~11 g/100 g) with moderate sodium (92 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of lemon tart; the high sugar content (24.6 g/100 g, exceeding the 10 g/100 g solid-food threshold) promotes insulin resistance and vascular inflammation, while the high saturated fat (~11 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness — limit to very small occasional portions only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 24.6 g/100 g (exceeds 20 g/100 g diabetes hard gate) and high refined carbohydrate load (43.9 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lemon tart is unsuitable for regular consumption in Type 2 Diabetes management; sugar at 24.6 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, and the refined pastry carbohydrate base (43.9 g/100 g total carbohydrate) causes significant postprandial glycaemic spikes — avoid or restrict to very small portions on rare occasions only.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~11 g/100 g from butter-dominant pastry and lemon curd, combined with high sugar (24.6 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemon tart poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its estimated saturated fat content of approximately 11 g/100 g (well above the 5 g/100 g threshold), which raises LDL cholesterol and promotes atherogenesis; the high sugar content additionally contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia — individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence only.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon tart is a classic Western dessert pastry consisting of a crisp, buttery shortcrust pastry shell filled with a smooth, tangy lemon curd made from lemon juice, lemon zest, eggs, butter, and sugar. The pastry base is typically blind-baked before the lemon curd filling is added and set, producing a contrast of textures — a crumbly, rich shell against a silky, intensely citrus-flavoured custard interior. Originating in French patisserie tradition (known as tarte au citron), lemon tart is widely consumed across Western Europe, Australia, Singapore, and other markets influenced by European baking. Per 100 g, lemon tart provides 374 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 19.57 g fat (predominantly saturated from butter, estimated ~11 g/100 g), and 43.9 g carbohydrate of which 24.6 g is sugar. Dietary fibre is 4.0 g/100 g and sodium is 92 mg/100 g. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content make this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension, and the saturated fat burden raises concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low GI boundary) based on analogous sweet shortcrust pastry tart data, though the glycaemic load per serving is high (24.1) due to the large carbohydrate content. Lemon tart is classified as a processed snack food in the pies and pastries subgroup and contains gluten, eggs, and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lemon tart?
Is lemon tart high in sugar?
What is the GI of lemon tart?
Is lemon tart suitable for hypertension?
Is lemon tart high in saturated fat?
Can I eat lemon tart if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11110017) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~11 g/100 g using USDA FoodData Central lemon tart reference as a secondary cross-reference; this is the only field not directly from HPB. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct lemon tart GI measurement exists in approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for lemon tart are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F11110017, and no complete micronutrient breakdown was available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly matching lemon tart entry as of 2026-04-16. This unavailability is consistent across all primary approved sources (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 1100537) consulted for this specific food item. Saturated fat of 11.0 g/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central reference for lemon tart (FDC ID 1100537) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweet shortcrust pastry tart data from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr, doi:10.1093/ajcn/nqab233); no direct lemon tart GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 55 is classified as low GI (≤55 threshold per standard GI classification); the glycaemic load per serving is nevertheless high (24.1) due to the large carbohydrate content (43.9 g/100 g). Lemon tart is a Western / French-origin food; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory multilingual naming requirements apply. The input dietary_tags included 'low_sodium' (sodium 92 mg/100 g is relatively low for a pastry) and 'high_fibre' (dietary fibre 4.0 g/100 g meets the Singapore HPB high-fibre threshold of ≥3 g/100 g for solid foods); both tags are retained as contextually valid. Ontology match_type values set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact lemon-tart-specific codes are confirmed in either system.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Lemon Tart', a precise canonical English name. No mandatory local-language names apply as this is a Western / French-origin food, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. G6 is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (374.0), protein_g (5.6), fat_g (19.57), carbohydrate_g (43.9) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Foodo F11110017 with confidence 0.95. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (4.0), sodium_mg (92.0), saturated_fat_g (11.0, estimated from USDA FDC cross-reference and documented in notes), sugar_g (24.6) all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low' — corrected from 'medium'; GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range ≤55 per standard classification), glycemic_load_per_serving (24.1), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr), a peer-reviewed journal, with estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent class (tart food product) and FoodEx2 closest_parent category (A00LC, tarts and flans) used as defensible anchors with honest match_type declarations ('closest_parent') and gap notes. No codes invented. SNOMED CT null with gap note. ontology_match_type values corrected to 'closest_parent' from 'broader' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Aliases flat array contains all strings from aliases_by_language (verified: 'Lemon Tart', 'Lemon tart', 'Lemon custard tart', 'Lemon curd tart', 'Lemon pie tart', 'Tarte au citron' all present in flat aliases). Alternate English names, French romanized form, and common variant names captured.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 fixes: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] — resolves g5_ontology_honesty gate failure.

Iteration 3 fixes: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per standard GI classification; this resolves q4_glycemic_completeness failure.

Iteration 3 fixes: Expanded notes field to explicitly document all -1 sentinel fields with reason for unavailability and citation of approved sources consulted (HPB Foodo F11110017 and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 1100537) — resolves g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure.
Iteration 3 fixes: Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify that GI 55 is classified as low GI and explain the high GL despite low GI.
Preserved all correct sections from iteration 2: core macros, secondary nutrients, health_context, llm_training, aliases, aliases_by_language consistency, nutrient_source, serving, classification.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Lemon Tart (F11110017) passes all hard gates and scores 8/8. Three issues from iteration 2 resolved: (1) ontology match_type values corrected to 'closest_parent' to satisfy allowed enum values, fixing g5; (2) gi_category corrected to 'low' consistent with GI 55 (≤55 threshold), fixing q4; (3) all -1 sentinel fields explicitly documented in notes with approved sources consulted, fixing g3. Core macros sourced from HPB Foodo with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FDC cross-reference and fully documented. All three required health conditions present with clinical guidance. Aliases consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language. No mandatory multilingual requirements apply for this Western-origin food.
